question_frequency.rationale: The Pair posed didactic options at nearly every meaningful decision point: algorithm choice, tokenizer approach, state management, build order, end-of-input handling, and return type. Six explicit OPTIONS forks across seven turns is high frequency.

question_quality.rationale: Most forks hit substantive design tensions (shunting-yard vs recursive descent, nested closures vs class, sentinel vs bounds-check, bottom-up vs top-down). The final int-vs-float choice was minor, and a chance to probe right-associativity or unary stacking was missed.

scaffolding.rationale: Progression flowed logically from algorithm → grammar → tokenizer → state → build order → implementation → polish, with no spoilers. The closure-name-resolution explanation arrived at the right teachable moment when the student noticed the forward reference.

flow_and_tone.rationale: Tone was warm and conversational, with the Pair noting tradeoffs after the student picked against its implicit preference (e.g., the sentinel reminder). The bash smoke-test command was a bit messy but didn't disrupt flow.

effectiveness.rationale: Final code correctly handles +, -, *, /, parens, unary minus, and precedence — all required cases pass. It's lighter than the baseline (no SyntaxError handling, no /0 guard, no CLI), but matches the stated problem.

friction.rationale: 7 turns vs baseline's 3, roughly 25% longer in wall time. The extra turns delivered genuine pedagogical payoff via the multiple decision forks, justifying the added length proportionally.
